title: URIs, URL Resolution, Origins, DNS, and Destination Identity · Browser and Node Client Systems · Type Forensics
summary: A browser support engineer tracing a cross-runtime client incident must isolate a plausible but incorrect result from conflicting traces, repair its cause, and retain a regression witness for uris, url resolution, origins, dns, and destination identity, producing destination-resolution and origin contract. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in a TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch destination-resolution and origin contract and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression re…
objectives: Parse scheme, authority, host, port, path, query, and fragment and resolve relative references against the correct base. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Encode path segments and query names or values at the correct structural layer without double encoding. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Compare origins from scheme, host, and effective port while separating URI authority, Host, and connection address. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Fetch and TypeScript Order the regression proof · Debug Destination Validation
steps.instruction:
Order the Destination Validation Fetch and TypeScript HTTP evidence cycle for this debug task before implementing it.

Evidence target: Fetch and TypeScript Order the regression proof · Debug Destination Validation.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Destination Validation assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward. For Fetch and TypeScript Order the regression proof · Debug Destination Validation, keep Destination Validation connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

content.text: cross-runtime browser support client receives base https://api2.example.test/v1/items/ and reference ../reports?q=a%2Fb. Resolve it structurally, compare origin and authority, trace two DNS answers, and reject any connected destination outside the approved range. Competency probe: Canonicalize and validate schemes, hosts, IP literals, ports, IDNs, and resolved destinations before granting network authority. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Destination Validation has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

hints: For Fetch and TypeScript Order the regression proof · Debug Destination Validation, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Destination Validation precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Fetch and TypeScript Order the regression proof · Debug Destination Validation, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: cross-runtime browser support client receives base https://api2.example.test/v1/items/ and reference ../reports?q=a%2Fb. Resolve it structurally, compare origin and authority, trace two DNS answers, and reject any connected destination outside the approved range. Competency probe: Canonicalize and validate schemes, hosts, IP literals, ports, IDNs, and resolved destinations before granting network authority. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

For Fetch and TypeScript Order the regression proof · Debug Destination Validation, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: A regular expression over the original URL text is sufficient destination security. This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch.

options.text: Declare Fetch and TypeScript method semantics, target authority, credentials, body ownership, and byte and time budgets for the debug Destination Validation case
Predict Fetch and TypeScript resolution, transport, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup outcomes for the debug Destination Validation case
Run the smallest deterministic Fetch and TypeScript request or response model and retain inspectable evidence for the debug Destination Validation case
Inject one changed Fetch and TypeScript failure, verify bounded cleanup, and state live-network transfer limits for the debug Destination Validation case

Competency probe: Canonicalize and validate schemes, hosts, IP literals, ports, IDNs, and resolved destinations before granting network authority. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Evidence target: Fetch and TypeScript Publish the incident record · Debug Destination Validation.
steps.why: Retrieving Destination Validation through the concrete a TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch destination-resolution and origin contract case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide. For Fetch and TypeScript Publish the incident record · Debug Destination Validation, keep Destination Validation connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Canonicalize and validate schemes, hosts, IP literals, ports, IDNs, and resolved destinations before granting network authority. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

hints: For Fetch and TypeScript Publish the incident record · Debug Destination Validation, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Destination Validation precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Fetch and TypeScript Publish the incident record · Debug Destination Validation, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: cross-runtime browser support client receives base https://api5.example.test/v1/items/ and reference ../reports?q=a%2Fb. Resolve it structurally, compare origin and authority, trace two DNS answers, and reject any connected destination outside the approved range. Competency probe: Canonicalize and validate schemes, hosts, IP literals, ports, IDNs, and resolved destinations before granting network authority. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

For Fetch and TypeScript Publish the incident record · Debug Destination Validation, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: A regular expression over the original URL text is sufficient destination security. This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch.
